synthesis.summary: CarMax issued a warning about persistent low margins in the used-car market, dragging Carvana down 9.2% on contagion fears — investors treating the peer's margin guidance as a read-through to CVNA's own profitability outlook. The move's magnitude (~9%) exceeds what a peer's guidance warning would typically justify, suggesting broader selling pressure or options activity is amplifying the decline.

HOOD is up 11.3% on elevated volume (1.83x relative), but the only available news is a Benzinga article asking why the stock is rising — not explaining it. With RSI at 71 and a 25% 5-day run, this looks like momentum continuation or options-driven flow, but no supplied news item identifies a concrete catalyst such as earnings, guidance, or a product announcement.

META dropped nearly 5% on elevated volume with no company-specific news in the supplied set to explain the move. The only related item covers SNAP's smart glasses pricing reception, which is a competitor product story and does not directly implicate META's financials or outlook. The decline likely reflects broader market pressure, sector rotation, or options/macro flows not visible in the provided news.

AT&T dropped 3.5% on elevated volume after CFO Pascal Desroches announced his retirement, creating leadership uncertainty at a senior financial level. The move is proportionally large for a CFO departure at a mega-cap telecom, suggesting the market views Desroches' exit as a meaningful loss or is pricing in transition risk around capital allocation and financial strategy.

KMX dropped 9% after issuing a warning that low margins will persist, signaling the used-car pricing environment or cost structure isn't improving as expected. The guidance-driven margin warning directly triggered the selloff, with volume running at 2.3x normal confirming broad institutional reaction.

NBIS is up ~10% today and nearly 37% over the past five days, but the only available news item is a Benzinga "why is it surging?" article — a reactive piece that explains nothing about the underlying catalyst. With RSI at 70 and relative volume only modestly elevated at 1.15x, this move is not explained by any supplied news; options flow, short covering, or sector rotation are plausible but unverifiable here.

Snap fell 6.6% after Wall Street pushed back on the pricing of its newly announced smart glasses, signaling skepticism that the product can achieve meaningful consumer adoption at its current price point. The concern centers on addressable market size being constrained by cost, not the product concept itself. Elevated relative volume suggests broader selling pressure beyond a single analyst note, so the news explains the direction but likely not the full magnitude of the drop.

INTC is up 4.3% today and 14.1% over five days, but the only supplied news item is a low-impact generic sector sweep with no INTC-specific catalyst. The move — on below-average relative volume (0.86) — is not explained by available news and likely reflects options flow, sector rotation, or a catalyst not captured in the provided feed.
